108:1.h1_en: I'rab of Surah Al-Kawthar Ayah 1 — word-by-word Arabic grammar
108:1.tldr_en: The verse is an emphatic nominal sentence built on the particle inna. Its written form إِنَّآ fuses inna with the attached pronoun nā, which serves as inna's noun (its subject). The predicate is the verbal clause aʿṭaynāka l-kawthar, where the verb carries two objects: the addressee pronoun ka and the noun al-kawthar.
108:1.translation_en: Indeed, We have granted you the abundance (al-Kawthar).

words_en.note_en: This single written form combines the emphatic particle inna, which governs a nominal sentence, with the attached pronoun nā ("We") that functions as its noun in the accusative position.

words_en.role_en: perfect verb with attached subject pronoun, forming the predicate (khabar inna)
words_en.note_en: A past-tense verb fixed on a vowelless ending because it is joined to the subject pronoun nā ("We"), and the whole verbal clause stands as the predicate of inna.
The attached second-person pronoun "you" sits in the accusative position as the first object of the doubly-transitive verb aʿṭā.
This noun is the second object of the verb, marked accusative by the visible fatha on its final letter.

108:1.irab_en: The ayah is an emphatic nominal sentence introduced by inna. In writing, إِنَّآ merges two elements: the particle inna, which puts what follows into the accusative and gives emphasis, and the suffixed pronoun nā ("We"), which serves as the noun of inna and occupies the accusative position. The predicate of inna is the verbal clause that follows. أَعْطَيْنَا is a perfect verb built on a quiescent ending because it attaches to the subject pronoun nā, the doer of the action. The verb aʿṭā takes two objects: كَ, the attached pronoun "you," stands as the first object in the accusative position, and ٱلْكَوْثَرَ is the second object, shown accusative by the fatha on its end. The verbal sentence as a whole functions as the predicate (khabar) of inna.

faqs_en.q: Why is the word إِنَّآ written as one piece, and what are its grammatical parts?
faqs_en.a: It is the emphatic particle inna joined in writing to the attached pronoun nā ("We"). Inna provides emphasis and governs the sentence, while nā is its noun (ism inna) and holds the accusative position. The verbal clause after it serves as inna's predicate.

faqs_en.q: Why does the verb aʿṭā take two objects (ka and al-kawthar)?
faqs_en.a: The verb ʿaṭā (to give/grant) is doubly transitive, meaning it naturally requires two objects: the recipient and the thing given. Here ka ("you") is the first object and al-kawthar ("the abundance") is the second, both in the accusative — ka by position and al-kawthar by the visible fatha.

faqs_en.q: What is the predicate (khabar) of inna in this verse?
faqs_en.a: The predicate is the entire verbal sentence aʿṭaynāka l-kawthar. When inna's predicate is a verbal clause, that clause as a whole fills the predicate slot, even though it has no single case ending of its own.

108:2.h1_en: I'rab of Surah Al-Kawthar Ayah 2 — word-by-word Arabic grammar
108:2.tldr_en: This verse is built on two parallel commands. The connector fa- ("so") links it to the previous ayah, then comes the imperative verb salli ("pray"), whose object of devotion is marked by the prepositional phrase li-rabbika ("for your Lord"). The conjunction wa- joins a second imperative, inhar ("and sacrifice"), sharing the same hidden "you" subject.
108:2.translation_en: So pray to your Lord and sacrifice.

words_en.note_en: This prefixed particle 'fa-' links the command to the previous verse, carrying the sense of a result or consequence ('so, therefore').
An imperative verb built on the deletion of its weak final letter, with an obligatorily hidden subject pronoun meaning 'you' (the Prophet).

words_en.role_en: prepositional phrase (jarr wa-majrur) attached to the verb
words_en.note_en: The preposition 'li-' ('for/to') governs 'rabb' in the genitive, and the phrase connects to the verb salli to show for whom the prayer is performed.
The attached pronoun 'ka' ('your') is in the genitive position as the second term of the construct with rabb.
The conjunction 'wa-' ('and') joins the following imperative to the verb salli.
A sound imperative verb built on a quiescent ending, with an obligatorily hidden 'you' subject, coordinated with salli.

108:2.irab_en: The verse opens with the particle fa-, a connector that ties the command to the gift announced in ayah 1, giving the sense "so/therefore." It is followed by salli, an imperative verb; because its root ends in a weak letter, its command form is built on dropping that letter, and its subject is a concealed pronoun "you," addressing the Prophet. The phrase li-rabbika consists of the preposition li- ("for"), the noun rabb in the genitive, and the attached pronoun ka ("your") functioning as the possessor; this whole prepositional phrase attaches to salli, clarifying for whom one prays. The conjunction wa- then links a second command, inhar ("and sacrifice"), a sound imperative built on a quiescent ending whose subject is likewise a hidden "you." The two imperatives stand in parallel, sharing one addressee.

faqs_en.q: Why does the verb صَلِّ end the way it does instead of with a normal sukun?
faqs_en.a: Its root letter at the end is a weak (vowel-like) letter. For such verbs the imperative is formed by deleting that weak final letter, so صَلِّ ends in a kasrah-marked stem rather than a plain quiescent consonant, unlike the sound imperative ٱنْحَرْ.

faqs_en.q: What does the prefixed فَ at the start of the verse do grammatically?
faqs_en.a: It is a connective particle, not part of the verb. It links this command to the previous verse and conveys a sense of consequence ('so/therefore'): because of the abundance God has given, one is told to pray and sacrifice.

faqs_en.q: Where is the subject of 'pray' and 'sacrifice'?
faqs_en.a: Both imperatives have no visible subject. Arabic imperatives carry an obligatorily hidden pronoun meaning 'you' (anta), here directed at the Prophet, so no separate word for the doer appears.

108:3.h1_en: I'rab of Surah Al-Kawthar Ayah 3 — word-by-word Arabic grammar
108:3.tldr_en: This verse is a nominal sentence opened by the emphatic particle inna. Inna governs shani'aka as its accusative noun (ism inna), with the attached pronoun -ka in the genitive as a possessive. The pronoun huwa serves as a separating pronoun (damir fasl), and al-abtar is the nominative predicate (khabar inna).
108:3.translation_en: Indeed, the one who hates you — he is the one cut off (left without lasting legacy).

words_en.note_en: Inna is a particle of emphasis that puts the following noun in the accusative and the predicate in the nominative.
Shani' is the accusative noun governed by inna, marked by the visible fatha, and it is an active participle meaning the one who hates.
The attached pronoun -ka is built on fatha and stands in a genitive position as the possessor of shani'.
Huwa is a separating pronoun with no grammatical case; it adds emphasis and distinguishes the predicate from a mere adjective.
Al-abtar is the nominative predicate of inna, marked by the visible damma, meaning the one cut off without continuation.

108:3.irab_en: The verse is an emphatic nominal sentence. It begins with inna, a particle that strengthens the statement, governs the following noun in the accusative, and keeps the predicate in the nominative. Shani'aka is the noun of inna (ism inna), accusative with a visible fatha; it is an active participle ("the one who hates/detests you"), and the attached pronoun -ka is built on fatha occupying a genitive slot as the possessor. The pronoun huwa is a separating pronoun (damir fasl); it carries no case role and serves to emphasize the predicate and signal that what follows is a true predicate rather than an adjective. Al-abtar is the predicate of inna (khabar inna), nominative with a visible damma, conveying "the one cut off" from goodness and lasting remembrance.

faqs_en.q: Why is shani'aka in the accusative case?
faqs_en.a: Because it is the noun of inna (ism inna). The particle inna places the noun that immediately follows it into the accusative case, so shani' takes a visible fatha as its accusative marker.

faqs_en.q: What is the function of the pronoun huwa here?
faqs_en.a: Huwa is a separating pronoun (damir fasl). It has no case role of its own; instead it adds emphasis and clarifies that the following word al-abtar is the predicate of the sentence rather than an adjective describing shani'aka.

faqs_en.q: What case is al-abtar, and why?
faqs_en.a: Al-abtar is nominative, shown by the visible damma, because it is the predicate of inna (khabar inna). While inna makes its noun accusative, it leaves the predicate in the nominative.
